text: So this is another in our series of what I would consider advanced robotic lobectomies. These are lobectomies in patients that have complete fissures. So it's a thick, complete fissure, and you have to be able to handle that. This seems to cause folks the most challenges as they're learning how to do robotic lobectomies.

This is going to be a left upper lobectomy with an additional challenge, and that is the patient has a patent left internal mammary artery graft. He had prior bypass surgery The lima was going to the LAD. It was patent. He needed it.

And I think the da Vinci is a great way to do these procedures. It clearly has advantages over vats for this type of procedure because you can dissect the lima so cleanly away from the left upper lobe.

Tom D'Amico wrote this paper that's cut in here that we actually left a wedge of lung parenchyma attached to the lima to avoid having to dissect it cleanly away from the lobe. And he said the survival is the same, but intuitively, if you can dissect the whole lobe away from the lemma and you don't have to leave behind any of that pulmonary parenchyma from the lobe harboring the tumor attached to the lemma, that would be preferable. It's not a compromise operation.

The surgeon demonstrates the port setup for a left upper lobectomy using robotic assistance. Four robotic ports are utilized, including twelve millimeter ports for the stapler positioned anteriorly and posteriorly, with the posterior port providing optimal access to the left upper lobe vein and the first branch of the pulmonary artery. An eight millimeter camera port is placed at the junction between the sternum and xiphoid, aligned with the sternal notch and extending to the seventh intercostal space.

The surgeon carefully inserts a blunt black plastic obturator into the left chest, navigating through potential adhesions from previous mammary artery procedures. A VATS technique is employed to take down adhesions, facilitating the placement of four robotic ports and a twelve millimeter assistant port at the junction between the diaphragm and thorax.

The surgeon proceeds with the resection, encountering numerous adhesions in the chest cavity. Using bipolar cautery, the adhesions are lysed while avoiding the lung parenchyma to prevent air leaks. The camera is initially positioned in arm two, with the cautery in arm one and bipolar in arm three. The camera is then switched to arm three for a medial view, repositioning the cautery to arm two and the long bipolar to arm four.

The surgeon performs a bottom-to-top dissection, starting inferiorly to separate the lung from the left internal mammary artery (LIMA). The camera is repositioned to optimize the view for this unconventional approach, highlighting the versatility of the XI system in switching camera ports.

The surgeon carefully dissects the remaining adhesions between the lung and the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) near the chest wall, using wristed instruments under ten times magnification for precision. The proximity and accuracy of the dissection are visually evident.

So our consult time or, you know, trocars into trocars out time is usually about This case took us, um, a hundred and thirty minutes, trochars in to trochars out, so it was about forty, forty-five minutes longer than normal. Um, but I would add at least half an hour, maybe an hour, um, if you're going to, uh, dissect it away from the lima, or you have adhesions, um, depending on how fast you operate. But, um, but just be patient and do a good, uh, safe dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects away from the lung parenchyma using robotic arms to prevent air leaks. The camera is positioned in arm three for a lateral view, enhancing visibility of the lemograph. A fourth instrument, a tip-up, is added to arm one, allowing control of two instruments with the left hand. The long bipolar grasper is in arm four, facilitating precise manipulation of tissues.

The surgeon uses a lateral camera position to dissect the left upper lobe of the lung away from the Lima, revealing surgical clips on the Lima. The bipolar cautery is set at eight, ensuring precise energy application between the tips. A sponge roll is used to gently push on the lung tissue, facilitating the dissection process.

The surgeon uses two sponge rolls to perform blunt dissection and bipolar cautery to separate the left upper lobe of the lung from the chest wall. The dissection is progressing towards the area where the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) is expected to emerge.

Get in close, use the articulated instruments, dissect it right off the Lima. I've probably done ten or twelve of these now, and I think it's a very safe way to do this dissection. I think you have to be patient. I think it takes a little bit of time. It doesn't take all day. It takes a little bit of time. Here, I think we're getting very close to the lima, so you can see I'm going slow. Incidentally, this video, I kind of toggle back and forth between two-time speed and four-time speed. I think to look at the whole thing in two-time speed, which is what I used to use, is a little tedious. People ask me, can you make it go a little faster? Can you be, you know, people don't have all day to watch these things, and I get it.

The surgeon approaches the Lima, revealing surgical clips and briefly entering the pericardium. The left upper lobe is nearly free from adhesions, allowing for the start of the left upper lobectomy. The superior pulmonary vein becomes visible as the camera is repositioned to arm two. The surgeon uses the long bipolar in arm three and the tip-up in arm four to divide the inferior pulmonary ligament up to the inferior pulmonary vein, preparing to dissect the posterior hilum.

The surgeon dissects the posterior hilum, targeting the posterior mediastinal lymph nodes, including station seven and subcarinal nodes. The inferior pulmonary vein is visible as the lowest structure, with the left lower lobe bronchus and interlobar pulmonary artery positioned above it. This creates a clear landing zone for the staplers to be used later.

The surgeon uses a zero-degree camera to dissect the posterior hilum while the assistant holds the lung medially. The fourth arm pushes on the superior segment with a sponge roll to avoid injuring the left lower lobe and prevent air leaks. The surgeon attempts to lengthen the hilum for increased mobility, addressing adhesions that may be tethering the lung.

The surgeon separates the inferior pulmonary vein from the superior pulmonary vein, dissecting a hilar lymph node located between them. The dissection is slightly bloody, and the surgeon adjusts the approach to improve lung mobility by lengthening the hilum.

The surgeon carefully dissects the superior pulmonary vein away from the left upper lobe bronchus, encountering some bleeding while addressing the left-sided hilar lymph nodes. The superior side of the superior pulmonary vein is exposed, revealing the truncus anterior branch of the left pulmonary artery. A vessel loop is placed around the superior pulmonary vein for identification, but it is not divided at this stage.

The surgeon identifies the lingular branch of the superior pulmonary vein and ensures it is included in the dissection. The superior pulmonary vein is observed to have three segmental branches, including a notably wide branch. The surgeon uses a long curved bipolar instrument to maneuver around the branches. The preference is to divide the pulmonary artery branches before the pulmonary veins to minimize vascular congestion and bleeding during fissure dissection.

The surgeon has dissected the superior, anterior, and posterior hilum and is now examining the fissure. The thick fissure does not reveal the yellow of the pulmonary artery, indicating difficulty in locating it. The surgeon decides to focus on the isolated superior pulmonary vein and its three segmental branches, using a Rob Nell catheter for further manipulation.

The surgeon uses a Rob Nell catheter to encircle the superior pulmonary vein, preparing to divide it with a stapler. The stapler, loaded with either a gray or white cartridge, is positioned across the vein, aided by the catheter to ensure proper alignment. The stapler is articulated and clamped across the vein, completing the division.

The surgeon considers performing a front-to-back dissection, similar to a VATS lobectomy approach, due to the patient's thick fissure. This approach allows for flexibility in addressing the lobe based on anatomical challenges, avoiding the risk of creating air leaks by dissecting through the fissure.

The surgeon identifies the truncus anterior and the left upper lobe bronchus, dissecting the underside of the truncus anterior away from the bronchus. A lymph node is often present in this area. The Rob Nell catheter is used to find and dilate a small hole, protecting the arch of the aorta on the other side.

The surgeon prepares to take the truncus anterior using a stapler with a gray load, positioned through the posterior or lateral staple port. The superior pulmonary vein and the first branch of the pulmonary artery are accessed from the back, with the stapler articulating for a straight shot across the truncus anterior.

The surgeon attempts to isolate the left upper lobe bronchus due to the thick fissure, revealing a large lymph node located between the upper and lower lobe bronchi on the left side, identified as a station X lymph node.

The surgeon identifies and excises the station ten lymph node located between the upper and lower lobe bronchi. The bronchus is hugged closely, with the pulmonary artery branch visible on the opposite side.

I think we just got into a bronchial vessel there. My assistant comes in and out with the sucker. You know, I know some people do these cases without an assistant port, which I think is crazy, quite frankly. Why would you not use, why would you not use an assistant? You know, I mean, I don't care who it is. I mean, I have nice assistants, they're good, but even if you don't have a great assistant, you know, somebody can put a sucker in there and help you a little bit, retract a little bit.

The surgeon manipulates the left upper lobe bronchus, with the pulmonary artery visibly curling behind it. Using a tip-up instrument, the surgeon carefully pushes on the bronchus while retracting the lung on either side with a fourth arm. The second tip-up instrument, characterized by its long, blunt, sweeping curve, is employed to safely maneuver around the large structures.

The surgeon navigates around the bronchus, pulling a red rubber catheter retrograde to ensure space for the stapler. The pulmonary artery is visible just to the right and behind the bronchus, while the catheter remains in a safe plane for firing.

The surgeon uses a green load stapler to divide the left upper lobe bronchus, ensuring a tight fit across the bronchus. The stapler is positioned safely, and the surgeon checks that no suction catheter is in the airway before firing the stapler.
The surgeon has divided the bronchus, revealing the pulmonary artery. They assess for additional branches, noting that small branches may be secured with metal clips instead of staples.
The surgeon pulls the red rubber catheter retrograde around a sizable pulmonary artery branch, ensuring enough space to position the stapler across the branch in the tight surgical field.

So again, this is very similar to how I used to do vatsalobectomies. It's how Dr. McKenna taught me how to do them a while back. It's a front-to-back approach, and you can certainly do it. This isn't my preference. My preference is to open the major fissure and maybe get the lingular branch and maybe the posterior ascending branch in the fissure. I just think the case goes smoother that way, and you don't have to fire that blind, you know, around the left upper lobronchus, that blind pass.

which always makes me just a little bit, a little bit nervous. Incidentally, that feeling you get when you dissect these pulmonary arteries, you know, when it's real, they're real tight and it's just kind of, it feels kind of dicey. It's like, oh my goodness, I hope these things don't start bleeding. Every thoracic surgeon feels that. I don't care how many of these you've done. It's all, I mean, there's sort of an exhilaration You know, as you dissect these little pulmonary artery branches, you know the stakes of it. You know how thin the margin for error is. And I think that's what makes these lobectomies awesome cases.
Every lobectomy is like a puzzle. Everyone's unique. And They never get boring. They never get routine. It's an awesome case.

The surgeon identifies a posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery, which appears bifurcated into two smaller branches. Both branches are secured using a gray load stapler. The bronchus, already divided, is held up with the fourth arm to create tension on the pulmonary artery for better access.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field for any remaining pulmonary artery branches, identifying connective tissue and a lymph node, which is being dissected away.

The surgeon begins dividing the thick major fissure using a stapler, with all vascular structures already separated. The clear plane of the fissure is visible, allowing for a systematic approach across it.

if you open up the the sixty surefire and you fire it three times you it's a wash financially you know it's a disposable stapler people don't like to open additional instruments but what I'm told by the experts in the economics of these things is that if you can fire the surefire sixty three times it's a wash and I would have fired it three times so we just have a little bit more parenchyma there and that's going to be that's going to detach the entire left upper lobe.

The surgeon uses their fourth arm to retract the lung upwards and out of the way, preparing for another staple fire. The bronchus is clamped without inflating the lung, maintaining clear anatomical orientation during the dissection.

The surgeon fires the stapler once more, aiming to minimize the number of staple fires by dissecting close to the hilum and taking small branches together. The hardware is being moved out of the surgical field.

The surgeon uses an extra-large purple anchor bag to collect tissue, carefully pulling the bag towards the feet to ensure it opens properly. The bag is inserted away from the hilum to avoid injury, while the surgeon completes the lymph node dissection, focusing on AP window lymph nodes.

The surgeon packs the surgical area with Surgicel, a hemostatic agent, indicating the completion of lymph node dissection across various stations. The Surgicel appears as a cotton-like material used to control bleeding.

The surgeon applies a TACOSIL fibrin sealant patch directly onto the staple line of the lung parenchyma, ensuring it is wet to adhere properly. The patch is left in place for three minutes to prevent air leaks. Concurrently, a 21-gauge Wang needle is used to inject bupivacaine into the intercostal spaces for a supplemental local block.

So that's a left upper lobectomy. It's a challenging one, but it's definitely one that is doable. And if you have any questions as always, please reach out to me through the contact information on my website. Thanks for watching.
